copy.hostHeadline: Surface, examine, and better understand how the everyday interactions of your team shape your culture.
copy.hostLede: Create a session, put the QR code on a screen, and everyone answers individually. All answers are anonymous. You'll get one individual score and a shared team score, including where you are now and where you want to go.
copy.playerLede: About {count} quick questions — some about how you'd respond, some about what usually happens on your team. It's completely anonymous, and it rolls up into one team result.

values.label: Courage
values.blurb: Saying the hard thing, even when it's easier to stay quiet.

values.label: Excellence
values.blurb: Holding the standard when no one's watching.

values.label: Respect
values.blurb: Treating everyone like they belong here.

values.label: Trust
values.blurb: Having each other's backs, on and off the floor.

values.label: Care
values.blurb: Looking out for each other, especially when someone's struggling.

values.label: Accountability
values.blurb: Owning mistakes instead of hiding them.

improvementTips.courage: Pick one small thing to name out loud this week — a comment that crossed a line, a teammate left out. Courage is a muscle; short reps build it.
improvementTips.excellence: Hold your own standard when it's inconvenient — the rep nobody checks, the cooldown after a loss. Standards spread from whoever keeps them first.
improvementTips.respect: Watch who goes quiet when the group gets loud. Make one deliberate move to pull them back in each session.
improvementTips.trust: Follow through on the small stuff (be on time, keep what's private private). Trust is built in reps, not speeches.
improvementTips.care: Notice who's having a rough one and check in — a quiet, private 'you good?' lands harder than any team speech.
improvementTips.accountability: When something goes wrong, name your own part before anyone asks. It makes it safe for everyone else to do the same.

questions.prompt: A first-year is getting mocked in the locker room — the gear they showed up in, the way they talk. They're half-laughing, looking at the floor. What do you actually do?

options.label: Laugh along — it's just how the room is
options.insight: Laughing along reads as endorsement. The target remembers who joined in.

options.label: Stay quiet and let it pass
options.insight: Silence avoids the conflict but doesn't change the norm — the room learns this is allowed.

options.label: Find them privately afterward to check in
options.insight: Private repair rebuilds the relationship, even if the moment itself went unchallenged.

options.label: Say something in the moment to shut it down
options.insight: Naming it live resets the norm for everyone watching — the highest-leverage move.

questions.prompt: You lose a close game and a teammate's mistake gets singled out loudly in the locker room. How do you respond?

options.label: Agree — it was clearly their fault
options.insight: Scapegoating dodges your own part and leaves a teammate alone with the loss.

options.label: Stay out of it
options.insight: Neutrality here still lets the blame stick.

options.label: Name your own part in the loss first
options.insight: Owning your piece first takes the heat off one person and lets the team move from blame to fixing it.

options.label: Shut the blame down — we win and lose together
options.insight: Protecting a teammate publicly is a trust deposit the whole room notices.

questions.prompt: Two starters skip the recovery session the staff set, calling it 'basically optional.' Younger players are watching to see if it really is. You…

options.label: Head out with them — it's not a big deal
options.insight: The standard is set by whoever the youngest players copy — and they're watching.

options.label: Do the session quietly, say nothing
options.insight: Leading by example works, but staying silent leaves the norm ambiguous.

options.label: Make it a team thing and pull people back in
options.insight: Turning the standard into a shared default is how culture actually holds.

questions.prompt: A transfer joined last week and keeps ending up on the edge of every group — bus, meals, warmups. What's your move?

options.label: Let them find their own way in
options.insight: Sink-or-swim inclusion mostly produces sinking — and a quiet outsider.

options.label: Be friendly if they come to you
options.insight: Passive warmth is better than nothing, but rarely closes the gap.

options.label: Deliberately bring them into the group
options.insight: Active inclusion is a small act with outsized effect on whether someone stays.

questions.prompt: The group chat is piling onto a teammate's bad-game clip. Reactions are rolling in. You…

options.label: Add a laughing reaction
options.insight: Pile-ons feel harmless in a chat but land hard on the person in the clip.

options.label: Mute it and move on
options.insight: Opting out personally doesn't change what the group is doing.

options.label: DM the teammate something supportive
options.insight: A private check-in repairs the person even when you can't move the group.

options.label: Redirect the thread to something else
options.insight: Changing the energy without a callout often works better than a lecture.

questions.prompt: Your best player sets a bad tone — cutting comments, eye-rolls at teammates — but the team keeps winning. Do you address it?

options.label: Leave it — we're winning, don't rock the boat
options.insight: Letting results excuse behavior teaches everyone the standard is negotiable for stars.

options.label: Vent about it to other teammates
options.insight: Side conversations erode trust without changing anything.

options.label: Talk to them directly, one-to-one
options.insight: Direct, private, specific. The only version of this that actually lands.

questions.prompt: A teammate confides they've been struggling off the court and asks you to keep it quiet. Later, others are guessing why they've been 'off.' You…

options.label: Share a little — they'd understand
options.insight: 'A little' is still a breach. Confidentiality is the whole point of being trusted.

options.label: Deflect and keep it to yourself
options.insight: Holding a confidence under pressure is exactly how trust is earned.

options.label: Tell them the team is asking, let them decide
options.insight: Giving them control over their own story respects both the secret and the person.

questions.prompt: A 'tradition' for first-years is framed as bonding, but it's clearly making a couple of them uncomfortable. Most people treat it as normal. You…

options.label: Go along — it's tradition, everyone did it
options.insight: 'Tradition' is how harmful norms survive. Discomfort is the signal to stop.

options.label: Quietly opt out yourself
options.insight: Personal opt-out is a start but leaves the first-years exposed.

options.label: Speak up or flag it to someone who can change it
options.insight: Escalating appropriately protects the people who can't push back themselves.

questions.prompt: You get bumped from the starting lineup for a teammate. It stings. How do you carry it around the team?

options.label: Let it show — cold shoulder, less effort
options.insight: Punishing the team for a coaching decision corrodes both standards and relationships.

options.label: Keep it professional but distant
options.insight: Professionalism holds the floor, but distance still costs the connection.

options.label: Support the starter and keep your standard up
options.insight: Backing a teammate through your own disappointment is one of the loudest culture signals there is.

questions.prompt: When someone makes a visible mistake in a game, what usually happens next on your team?

options.label: It gets pinned on them and it lingers
options.insight: When mistakes stick to a person, people stop taking the risks that win games.

options.label: Coaches handle it; teammates stay out of it
options.insight: Leaving it to staff is fine, but the team isn't owning its culture either way.

options.label: We own it as a group and move on
options.insight: Shared ownership is what lets a team fail forward instead of fracturing.

questions.prompt: A new player joins mid-season. On your team, they usually…

options.label: Have to earn their place before anyone bothers
options.insight: 'Earn it first' cultures lose good people before they ever get going.

options.label: Get left to figure it out on their own
options.insight: Sink-or-swim isn't neutral — it quietly tells newcomers they're on the outside.

options.label: Get pulled in quickly by a few people
options.insight: A couple of intentional people deciding 'you're one of us' changes everything.

questions.prompt: Your team's group chat is mostly…

options.label: Banter that sometimes has a target
options.insight: When the jokes need a target, somebody's always quietly bracing for their turn.

options.label: Pretty quiet — logistics only
options.insight: Not harmful, but the off-court connection that carries teams isn't being built either.

options.label: Hype, plans, and looking out for each other
options.insight: The chat is a culture tell — this one builds belonging instead of pecking order.

questions.prompt: When something's off between teammates, on your team it usually…

options.label: Gets talked about — just not to the person
options.insight: Talking around people instead of to them is how trust quietly drains out of a room.

options.label: Gets buried until it blows up
options.insight: Avoidance doesn't resolve tension; it just compounds it until it costs you a game or a person.

options.label: Gets raised directly, even when it's awkward
options.insight: Teams that can be direct without it becoming personal handle adversity far better.

questions.prompt: When the team is tired and no coach is watching, the standard usually…

options.label: Drops — effort gets quietly optional
options.insight: The standard you keep unsupervised is the real one. Everything else is a performance for staff.

options.label: Depends entirely on who's in the room
options.insight: If the standard rides on specific people, it isn't a team standard yet.

options.label: Holds, because people hold each other to it
options.insight: Peer-held standards are the most durable kind — they don't leave when the coach does.
